summary: George Hotz writes an unusually reflective and optimistic post, deliberately stepping back from his typical critical stance to appreciate the current moment. He celebrates the democratization of AI with powerful models running on laptops and open-source research leading the cutting edge. He expresses satisfaction with his companies comma.ai and tinygrad, their sustainable business models, and their missions toward a decentralized future. He also finds positivity in emerging subcultures, accessible education, the US-China multipolar world being livable, and his personal life, while acknowledging that honest criticism of problems is still necessary.
key_points: Machine intelligence has reached a remarkable inflection point where frontier-class AI runs on consumer hardware and the best research is open source, making the field more competitive and accessible than ever.
Comma.ai and tinygrad represent a vision of a decentralized, post-cloud future where people actually own their technology, and both operate sustainably by selling hardware at a profit.
The multipolar world of US and China, despite their flaws, offers genuinely good quality of life on both sides — a fact easily lost in constant criticism.
Honest confrontation with problems is the necessary path forward in politics and society, preferable to pretending everything was fine.
Real education is more accessible than ever through channels like math YouTube, even as fake education proliferates — and maintaining the distinction matters.
money_quotes: We are alive to see the birth of machine intelligence.
The best AI research is open source, constraint is the mother of creativity.
The cutting edge is more open and competitive than ever.

I think the core missions of comma and tinygrad deeply shape our future in a decentralized post-cloud world where you actually own things, but the jury is still out. It wouldn't be fun if it was a sure thing.
The only way out is through, and the first steps are talking honestly about the problems, not continuing to act like everything was okay.
Like sure I'll complain about the 5 hyperscalers with the huge computers but I'd be lot more upset if there were no huge computers.
It's easier to fool yourself with fake education than ever, but it's also easier to get real education.
I learned very early that you have to separate your online persona from your inner person, so you don't get to hear about this stuff.
key_insight: Despite legitimate reasons for criticism, the current moment — with open-source AI on laptops, sustainable tech companies, vibrant subcultures, and accessible education — is genuinely remarkable and worth appreciating.
